speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 19, 1996. The survivor is Saul Gershkowitz-- G-E-R-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z. The interviewer is Carol Raspler, R-A-S-P-L-E-R. We are in Delray Beach in the Miamia, Florida district, USA. The language is English. And this is roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Carol Raspler. Today is March 19th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Saul Gershkowitz in Delray Beach, Florida, the United States of America. And we will be conducting this interview in English. Would you tell us your name, tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Saul Gershkowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell it, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an American, I spell it S-A-U-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gershkowitz. You said in American. How did you spell it in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Europe, they called me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which in Hebrew was Bezalel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bezalel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gershkowitz, the same way but it was spelled different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? How is it spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-C-Z-K-O-W-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerczkowcz, OK. And what was your name at birth, when you were born, Saul or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bezalel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bezalel. Did you ever have any nicknames? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They-- in the school, they called me 87. There was a, an animal. We used to collect pictures of animals in the candies. And the 87 was skunk. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A skunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a skunk. But they called you 87. Now what was your-- do you remember your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the date of birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know I was born. I took my own date of birth after the pogrom in, in Berezne and when we left alone and when I had to come down to the camps. So I-- I know that I was born between Pesach and Rosh Hashanah. So that means I took in the middle the day, the day, July 6th.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 6th. And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I'm 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: 70. And tell me, you said Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-Z-N-O, Berzno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berezne. That's where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were your parents born in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, my dad was born in Berezne. My mother was born in Sarny, which it was about 40 miles south from Berezne.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of 1996, what country are we speaking of?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1996, we're speaking of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berezne was, when I was born, it was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it still is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's-- in, in 1939, became Russia when Poland was divided into sections. Germany took half of it. And Russian came in to place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what province or district was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It, well, Berezne was a city, which it was Wolin-- W-O-L-I-N-- which it's a state. And the capital of Wolin was Rovno-- R-O-O-- umlaut. it's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-N-O, Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now for those of us unfamiliar with Polish geography but those of us who might know some really major cities or historically important cities, what would you say was the nearest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rovno was a very big city. Then it was Bialystok, which it was up north, and then Wilno, which it was up north, too. Krakow was south of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which was closest to Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Berezne?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of those cities.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno was the biggest city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next biggest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next biggest was Sarny-- S-A-R-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they anywhere near Krakow or Lublin or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bialystok.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystok-- no, no. They, they were-- Bialystok was maybe 230 miles. Rovno was about--

speaker: interviewer
text: North or south?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystock was north. Bialystock was north of Berezne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How close were you to the Austrian or, or Ukrainian or Russian border without-- before '39, just to give us a kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- during the First World War, Berezne was Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukraine. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to Germany, to Austria-- well, Austria, as far as I know, used to be-- they still owned, you know-- our place in Rovno was Austria, too, at one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah. It went back and forth through there. You know what, Saul, let's talk about life in Berezne in your family before the war. Now, how many were there in your family, brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- I had three brothers and two sisters. One brother died and then-- from a twin, which the twin was still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: As an infant, he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as an infant. And the other one died in birth. When after the war-- I mean, during, before the world war, world war, three brothers and two sisters in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Including you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Five children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your life like? What did your dad do in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad was a builder. And in the wintertime, he was a-- he did a lot of fancy carriages, wheels, all kinds of very fancy for, for a carriage, for a big, rich man if they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews or non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any really big, rich man in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Neighbor of mine by the name of [INAUDIBLE] was actually, I would say, at that time a rich man. He had-- used to supply all the flour and the corn and everything to bakeries. And he was considered a rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berezne was about 7,000 to 9,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a fairly small town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was about 3,500 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a third, more or less.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes half to a third. Uh-huh. And what was Jewish life-- what was your life like in Berezne as a, as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, it was normal life. My father worked. We had a nice home. We had a barn. And we had a shop for my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the wheels?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wintertime, he had a, a lay, a lay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lathe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which used to do the work. He had a lot of carpentry machinery. Most of it is hand. And then we had a barn for two cows. And we had a nice garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice piece of land, you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have people working for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had people working for him. One of them saved me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're going to ask you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: That story. And, and what was daily life like in Berezne? We'd like to know about Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily life? Well, in the morning, my mother used to go down and milk the cows. My father used to go down and bring the water, because the water had to be take out from a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about-- the well was about 200, 300 feet from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there toilets in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was outhouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outhouses.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two of them. We were rich. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to-- you had your meals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we had breakfast. And then I went to school, Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was breakfast like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breakfast? Well, sometimes my mother used to make a [POLISH], which made of dough with a little bit of put honey or maybe a little jam and a glass of milk right from the cow. The milk was still warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she used to make a sandwich. And then we came home. We-- when my father came home, we had supper together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left for school, you all went together? You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. There was no buses, no-- we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: School was about, I would say, 15 to 20 minutes' walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Winter, snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter, you walked. You put on nice, big gloves. And you walk. Dirt

speaker: interviewer
text: Roads or, or paved roads?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually hard clay. And the, the sidewalks are all made of wood, you know, planks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did they get muddy, those, those roads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, sure. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the springtime? And at night when you came home from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: So you do the homework. And you have supper. And you go to bed. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. No television.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't have a television at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school, was it a Jewish school, a Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a Polish school with plain teachers, no-- they had a Catholic schools separate. But this was a Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixed Jewish and Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed Jewish, Gentiles, yeah. And I used to go to cheder before, you know, before I had to have my bar mitzvah lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how old were the children in your first school, up to what age did it go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to nine, 10 grades-- nine grades or 10 grades, I think. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And didn't you play with all the children after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had, we had recess in school. We used to play football, whatever. And after school, we used to have get togethers with kids and play.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any-- ever any incidents of anti-Semitism? Or were there anything-- anything ever go on between the children, among the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see anything between the children. But we were on guard always, special before Easter. We had to be on guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why before Easter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they have the, the services. And they take out all the-- they carry all the plaques from the, from the goddess, from them. And, and they told us not to go out on the street at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the icons from the churches?

speaker: interviewee
text: The icons, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you? Your parents told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to go on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it could be arrest. You know, arrest some people. They, they tried to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] start fights. But as far as I know, we, we didn't go out. And till 1939, we-- I don't think it was too much anti-Semitism. It was anti-Semitism [INAUDIBLE]. But I didn't know about it, because I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the religious aspect of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my dad was religious. He wasn't pious. I mean, he didn't wear-- he wore tzitzits every day. I mean, I know. But there was more religious-- the rabbi and his, his followers were much more religious than my father. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was religious, yes. My mother came from a rabbinical family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was-- she was from Berezne as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was from Sarny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Sarny. You did say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her family was also from Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the best You know? They still live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met any grandparents. When I was born, my grandparents were already died. The only one we had is my mother's sister who lived in [INAUDIBLE]. And I know that my. my father's sister lived with us. And my father's brother and sister lived in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went, I think, in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Do you know why they specifically went? Do you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: They every talk to you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that my father had papers to come to America because a cousin of his by the name of [INAUDIBLE] send him the papers. And he never wanted to go by himself. And he didn't have papers for the-- for us and, and, and my mother. So he never went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- when did they send him those papers, long before?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the when, when that cousin, [INAUDIBLE], which he was a very successful businessman in America, was there for a few years, he did send them, I think, in 1920, '21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They couldn't get papers for the rest of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he, he's supposed to go by himself and then bring, then bring the family. And I don't think he wanted that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Tell me, to the best of your knowledge, what was the first sign of danger in Berezne from the war, from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sign is-- was 1939 in the, in the summer of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no inkling of what was going on in Germany or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were, we, we, we, we heard that Germany is, is-- took over Austria, took over Czechoslovakia. And they may, they may take over Poland. But we were way up north next to the Russian border, not far from Ukraine, which it was Russia at that time. And we saw a lot of people, Jews, who flew-- who came back from, from south, like Warsaw and-- to, to, to up north. And we heard stories about killings and pogroms. But you know, you always think that it's not going to happen to you. And till 1939, when they made up a pact with Germany and Russia, that Germany will take half of Poland and Russia will take half of Poland. And I was under the Russian-- city came under the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the family frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, frightened-- yes or no-- a little bit frightened. Because if you have, if you, you know, you were disturbed in your life, it's frightened. But you couldn't do anything. You thank God that you were under the Russian mandate. So it won't be any killing [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about the relationship between the Germans and the Jews specifically? Did, did that go around the town, that they were causing grief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, lately, you hear that, that they, they were causing a lot of, a lot of bad things for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what did you hear? Do you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to cut beads from the old Jewish people. They used to make fun of them in, in the middle of the street used to beat them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was rumors that you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing in Berezne at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, no, not at that time, no. Polish people were anti-Semites, Polish people. In the big cities, you can hear that happens a lot between the Polish people and the Jews, anti-Semitic wise. In our city, small city, most of it was-- very few Polish people there. Most of them were Ukrainian. The only one who was Polish is the guy who owned the whole city. And all the property was his.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean that, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- he was left with-- his grandfather and grandfather, they used to own hundreds of acres of land in the cities. They build the churches and everything. They used to own it. And then the people used to work for them. And they gave him so much back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for example, if your father was interested in buying acreage, he had to go to that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father bought the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: As an example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the, the land-- he had to go to that man and to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to agree on a price. And then he pay him, buy the land.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was sort of of one of these Polish noble family or something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noble families, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- I meant to ask you. What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of the time, we spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, they made us-- all the teachers made us speak Polish while we were in the Polish school. We used to have to put out the hand. And she, she took a piece of wood and-- because we spoke Yiddish to, to-- between us instead of Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't mean to skip over that. I just want to go back to this, as we approach the summer of '39 and the invasion, what did-- what had you heard about Nazism? Anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the only thing we hear that Germany is starting to take over a lot of countries. And that's all we heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the summer of '39? Let's see. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at that time in 1939, I was 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 14. Had you had your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A year after the bar mitvah, yes.

words.text: A year after the bar mitvah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had had your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what-- that was in shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your Shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Berezne shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did it look like, by the way? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a building, which its-- had a women's section upstairs and a man's a big hall with, with a nice altar with the, with the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Torahs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Torahs. We had a very, very famous rabbi by the name of Gedalche. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gedalche is G-E-D-A-L-C-H-E, I think. Gedalche, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- very famous. We never talk-- we never said the first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Rabbi Gedalche. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Berezne shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. As a matter of fact, I know that he came to Boston to raise money for a certain Jewish shul or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When did he come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he came down there in 1937 or '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the summer of '39, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians just-- they didn't have no opposition from the Polish. They just-- because the Polish army had only cavalry, because-- and they didn't-- they didn't put up any fight. I remember when this war started, they, they took my, my brother and my brother-in-law to the army, brother George. And the last thing we saw-- we know that after the war is over that he was in a prison in-- I mean, as a-- the Russians caught the whole army. And they sent them to Siberia, all of the Polish soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, in Berezne--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Poles came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poles mobilized--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mobilized.

speaker: interviewee
text: An army. They took my brother and my brother-in-law as Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. And they, they had them somewhere. And then the Russian army took him and took-- they didn't have too much fighting there. And they took him in prison. They send him to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they manage in Siberia? And how did you see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother was-- after they let him out from the prison camp, they, they gave him work in Russia. And he was in a Siberian city by the name of [INAUDIBLE]. It was, I think, 60 or 80 miles from, from Moscow, working in a hospital. And how did I-- how did we met him is-- I'll tell you later the story. But my brother, who was wounded in, in, in the Russian army, my younger brother-- I mean, my older brother but younger than the brother who was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he was wounded, he was in the same hospital. And when my brother came in and he looked at the names which had came in, and he saw Julius Gerszowicz. And that's how he met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two brothers met in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my god. And about what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We hear of these miracles. Now in Berezne, what was going on when Germany invaded Russia after September-- invaded Poland after September?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was, this was in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But up till then, it was a normal life. And then the Russians-- I mean, they, they took some of the businesses away from the big-- from the rich people. And they made it like stores for which everybody could, could go if he worked for the state. My father was workin', I mean, for himself. He still had people workin'. It was-- we went to school. They reduced my schooling from seven grade to six grade, because the Russian schools were better than the Polish schools. And we started to learn Russian. And it was going on normal for two years, till the start of the German war with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, about what time of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in the fall, in the fall, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? How was your life, your life changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the battle was actually-- and the Germans were on one side. And the Russians on the other side of Berezne. And when we went out-- we ran out of the house. And my father made, made us all lay in the ditch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of shooting-- and I would say 2/3 of the city burned down. Our house was burned down, too, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How quickly did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Overnight. I mean, the fight was going around maybe for six hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- give us that month again in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think was August.

speaker: interviewer
text: One. August of '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. And at that time, the morning the Russian were gone. And the German army startin' come in to our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- was there artillery over your house as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, artillery and rifle shots and machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the citizens, the everyday citizens, just sort of froze. They had nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowhere to go. You are in between. You on a battlefield. You are there on the line, worse that them. They had rifles. We lay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. You lay in the fields. And what, what happened, Saul? What happened after? You tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: After, after the Germans started to come in and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were laying in the fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fields-- well, we're laying. We saw, we saw the, the, the, the, the bullets flying back and forth. We hear them. But we're scared. And my, my mother was holding cover my daughter and me-- my, my sister and me. And my father was the, with the other brother. Because third brother was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll continue this on the next tape.
